summary:
Doxycycline can be used without dosage adjustment in cases of renal insufficiency because it primarily clears through non-renal routes such as biliary secretion; other common antibiotic classes rely critically upon normal glomerular filtration rates which necessitate reduced doses under impaired conditions. To summarize concisely while focusing only on critical aspects related directly back towards answering "All but one requires a change," we highlight how doxycyline stands out due to unique pharmacological properties allowing consistent effectiveness regardless of altered kidney functionality compared against alternatives needing careful monitoring/adjustment based purely off their reliance on intact nephron functions post-failure onset stages. Thus, our final answer remains unaltered at 'doxycycline'. Doxycycline doesn't typically demand dose adjustments despite compromised renal function since it mainly relies on hepatic metabolism rather than direct urinary excretion unlike several others
